explicit_text: I served in the Korean War as a Marine officer from around April 1952 to May 1953. I was part of the replacement draft and spent about eleven or twelve months there. The combat was tough, especially in the cold weather where North Koreans would attack at night, and we had to be ready with our weapons even while sleeping. I was assigned to the First Battalion, 5th Marines, Baker Company, and experienced frontline combat and rotations between line and reserve duty.

I was assigned to the First Battalion, 5th Marines, Baker Company, and experienced frontline combat and rotations between line and reserve duty.

entity: United States Naval Reserve Officers Training Corps
explicit_text: I joined the United States Naval Reserve Officers Training Corps, or NROTC, during my college years at Harvard University. The program included naval history and required summer active duty training each year under the Holloway Plan. In my sophomore year, I chose to serve in the Marine Corps instead of the Navy. The NROTC gave me valuable experience, including 90 days on active duty aboard the USS Macon and amphibious training at Little Creek, Virginia.

explicit_text: I chose to serve in the United States Marine Corps through the NROTC program and was commissioned as a second lieutenant, later promoted to captain in 1953. After graduating from Harvard, I attended the Marine Corps Basic School at Quantico and then trained at Camp Pendleton. I served in Korea with the First Battalion, 5th Marines, Baker Company, where I led a platoon in combat. The Marine Corps training was rigorous, including amphibious exercises and cold weather survival in the Sierra Nevada Mountains.

explicit_text: During the summer of 1948, while I was attending Harvard University, I served 90 days aboard the USS Macon as part of my NROTC summer training. My job was up on the mast as a pointer for the 8-inch guns, coordinating their elevation while another crew member turned the cranks to move them sideways. We practiced firing broadside salvos, and I remember how the ship would rock dramatically with each blast. It was a memorable experience that gave me insight into naval operations.

explicit_text: I attended Roxbury Latin School in West Roxbury, Massachusetts, which is the oldest continuously functioning secondary school in the country, founded in 1645. It was my secondary school before I went on to Harvard University. The school was a significant part of my early education, and I was fortunate to have scholarships that helped me along the way. My family valued education highly, as my father also benefited from scholarships at MIT.

entity: United States Marine Corps Basic School
explicit_text: After graduating from Harvard in 1951, I reported to the United States Marine Corps Basic School at Quantico for about 60 to 90 days of training. This was where I learned the fundamentals of being a Marine officer and prepared for leadership roles. From there, I was assigned to Camp Pendleton for further combat training before deploying to Korea. The Basic School was a critical step in my transition from student to Marine officer.

explicit_text: In the fall of 1951, I went to Camp Pendleton for combat training after completing the Basic School. At Pendleton, we also went to Pickel Meadows in the Sierra Nevada Mountains for cold weather training, where the snow was eight feet deep. This training was essential for preparing us for the harsh conditions we would face in Korea, including learning to manage frostbite risks with inadequate gear. Camp Pendleton was where I honed my skills as a platoon leader before deployment.

explicit_text: During my training and service in Korea, we did not have the insulated Mickey Mouse boots that were later issued. Instead, we wore old leather boots with just two pairs of socks, which often got wet and caused frostbite. We had to be creative, drying wet socks inside our uniforms to avoid freezing. The lack of proper cold weather gear like the Mickey Mouse boots made the Korean winters especially brutal for us.

This was part of our preparation to ensure we were proficient with our weapons before going into combat. The M1 Garand was a standard issue rifle, and maintaining it properly was critical for survival and effectiveness in the field.

explicit_text: On the way to Korea, our ship stopped overnight in Yokosuka, Kanagawa, Japan. We had some leave during that stop, and I remember going out and raising hell with the other Marines. It was a brief but memorable break before continuing on to Pusan and then up to the front lines in Korea. Yokosuka was a key staging point for us during the transit.

explicit_text: In Korea, I was assigned to the First Battalion, 5th Marines, which was part of the frontline combat units. Within this battalion, I served in Baker Company as a platoon leader. The men in the battalion, including seasoned veterans like Sergeant Vergopia, were instrumental in guiding me through my first combat experience.
